sentences.text: I forgot to mention that while we lived in the woods, the group of men decided they wanted to do away with the two children.
They were about six, seven years old.
Because they were in the way.
They were crying, they carried on, they were talking, they couldn't keep them quiet.
They were afraid because of the children they wouldn't survive.
So there was one man, Kaplan was his name.
He was a very learned man.
He-- he said to them, it's written.
It's written that if you save one, you save all.
And I wouldn't let you do away with them.
And they are alive today.
One lives in Canada, and one lives in New York.
Do you still keep in touch with them?
Yes, I'm in touch with them.
They're very dear to me.
Yes, yes, there were a few women.
There was this Doris Soroko with the daughter--
There were maybe five women, the rest men.
And this man that said, if we saved, one save all-- he was killed at the time when I was shot and wounded.
He was killed.
Yes, they killed him, yes.
They killed quite a few and some were able to escape.
They went to different woods.
Oh, I'm out of the hospital?
Yes.
We stayed-- yes, at that time, my husband will tell you in details about how he was arrested by the Russians, and he'll tell you all about it.
That was quite an incident.
And I was on crutches.
And I went to-- they came in during the night, and they arrested him because people squealed that he was working for the Germans.
But he'll tell you about it.
And I went on crutches to see my husband, and I went to speak to the major.
I went to speak to the head man.
At that time, I didn't know he was Jewish.
And I said, you arrested my husband.
We're survivors.
I told him the whole case history, and they let him out right away.
Yes.
I told him everything.
That we are Jewish.
How can be collaborating with the Nazis?
He worked-- I mean, with the Germans, he worked for them, but under assumed names.
He was supervising in the woods, which he'll tell you all about it.
And at that point, he befriended us.
And he gave us a wagon, and a horse and wagon, and-- and Russian soldiers, and he took us back to Ostrog, which was quite a distance.
And that's how we came to Ostrog.
Yeah, but I never went to live in that house because--
No.
No, no.
We just stayed in a different house.
We just a few left, and we stayed.
Right.
Yes.
Well, they were very good to us.
We were-- we were-- we were not Nazis.
We were not against them.
When I was in the hospital, we heard that the Germans defeated, and the Russians took over.
So that's when we were liberated.
Yes.
There were no more-- no more Nazis, no more Germans.
Yes.
And then we back together.
He was just in jail overnight, I think.
And then we went back to Ostrog, and then he went back to his house.
Did go back to his house.
And--
It was awful.
Was just horrendous.
I mean, we just-- we-- we didn't care at this point.
You don't care about a house, possessions.
You're happy to be alive.
And we were glad that we survived.
And then to some other people, there was a small group, and we became very close friends.
Then a month later, the Russians mobilized him.
He went to the army, and I was left alone.
That was the time that I came back.
I think I told you this.
Yeah.
Oh, I think we reached a point we were in Lourdes already, when I got a job as an interpreter.
Polish to Russian, right.
They arrested Polish underground.
They were resisting the-- the Russians.
They were enemies of Russia.
And when they-- they were caught also in the woods, they were hiding.
They brought them into a room and I interpreted their story.
And I had to write-- I questioned them in Polish, and I wrote everything in Russian.
Just the name, and address, and what they did before.
I really don't remember.
Everything was so chaotic at the time.
I was so scared, because in front of me I was left alone with this prisoner.
And I was sitting near a big window.
And then all of a sudden I'm looking down.
He jumped out of the chair and he broke the window and he fled.
He ran away.
And I don't think they ever caught him.
Nothing.
I just reported to them.
They knew what happened.
Well, it wasn't my fault, really.
And then I lived, I worked there.
And then my husband took a leave from the Russian army.
And then he'll tell you the rest of the story, how we both escaped from Poland, and how we went to Germany.
I don't remember the interpretation.
I-- one day my husband came, and he took a leave of absence.

He took a-- and then we found out through other Jewish people in Lourdes that they formed a contact, they had a contact with Israel, and they were taking Jews out from Poland to go to Germany-- to Austria first, then Germany, and then to Israel.
What's called, Aliyah Bet.
So they were-- in fact, this daughter, Soroko's husband, was very active in that.
And they told my husband to leave your gun, leave everything, and we're going to get you out.
And he said, fine.
And then he deserted the Russian army.
And I deserted my position, my job.
You just one day just didn't show up?
We just didn't show up, and they took us out, and they took us quick to-- to another city.
And then again, we were together with other Jewish people in the middle of the night by train.
We walked miles through forest.
They-- they bribed the guards.
There were Russian guards all over.
So this Aliyah Bet, they bribed the guards.
They let us go through in the middle of the night.
There were quite a few people.
And then we went to Austria through the border.
We went to Austria.
Everything by foot.
We went to Austria and we stayed there overnight.
And after that, we went to-- I forgot the name of the town.
And there we-- it was the Brit-- it was under occupation of the British at the time.
And they placed us in a big barrack.
But we were free.
They placed us in a big barrack.
And then we went for food.
We were on line for food.
It was an army barrack.
And the nights were just horrendous.
There were flying lice all over the place.
By the time-- by the way, I was full of lice all the time, all through the war.
My head, underarms, all over.
So they were-- I don't think they were called lice, and I forgot-- bed-- bed bugs.
They were not lice.
I had lice, but they were bed bugs.
And the minute they shut off the lights, they were all aiming just to me.
From the ceiling, and they landed on my body.
And I was bitten from head to toe, those bed bugs.
And I was scratching, and itching, and I was hysterical.
And I went by myself to the British captain, British major, and I cried, and I complained to him.
And I remember, I think he sent the exterminator.
They were very nice to us at the time.
Sent an exterminator.
It didn't help much, but I remember this incident, how it was bitten by bed bugs all over.
And after that, we went to Munich, and they placed us into a displaced person camp, Foehrenwald.
And we lived in one room with maybe 20 people.
And we were getting help from [NON-ENGLISH], from highers.
I think it was [NON-ENGLISH] at the time.
And we were on line for clothes which didn't fit, but we were glad to get any clothes.
And on line for food.
And my husband was playing the piano and entertained all the refugees in Foehrenwald.
And we just lived with hope that one day we'll go to America.
Yes.
We got married-- I forgot to mention.
We got married when we came back to Ostrog.
My husband and I, we came back to-- we got married in a civil-- civil--
Civil ceremony.
Yes.
Well, we were married in Ostrog when he-- before he was taken to the Russian army.
And then the second time we were married when we got to Munich, because we applied for papers to go to America.
I contacted my brother, my brothers.
And my husband contacted his brother.
And I had an uncle here, and they knew we were alive, and they were also sending care packages to Munich, to the-- to Germany.
Because Munich-- we moved from-- we stayed there just a short time in Foehrenwald.
Then we went to Munich, because my husband went to school.
So we were getting care packages.
That's how we be able to survive.
And then we have to get married again in Munich in order to apply for a visa to go to America.
And then I decided it wouldn't look nice for my relatives here that I don't have a religious ceremony.
So we decided to have a religious ceremony.
And at that time, there were no Polish religious people to perform the ceremony, but there were some Hungarians.
And they were so extreme.
I had to go to the Mikvah.
The woman dunked me under the water several times.
I thought she was going to drown me.
She said, I have to shave your hair.
I said, no way.
I said, I have to cut your nails.
I said, I'll let you do that.
I'll compromise.
And she never shaved my hair.
And then we waited from day to day to get a visa to go to America.
On a boat with other-- we're all refugees.
On a boat.
The name of the boat was Marine Perch.
I couldn't wait to go on the boat when I saw all the food.
And I went-- the first thing I got to the boat, I went to the dining room.
And I was shocked to see all the beautiful tables with tablecloths, and all the food that I hadn't seen in a long time.
Oh, they had fresh fruit, and vegetables, and everything.
And I ate like there is no end to it.
And right after we finished eating, there was a big storm, and everything went down.
And we were sick for 10 days we were on the boat.
And we were sick throwing up for 10 days.
I never thought I'll come out alive.
I said to one of the people, I said, you know what?
When I die here, they'll throw me in the ocean.
Then my brothers in America wouldn't know what happened to me.
But we survived.
The minute we saw the Statue of Liberty, it was like we reached heaven.
We cried.
Everybody cried.
And then my relatives were waiting for us, and that's a different story.
I learned English in school.
When I went to the [NON-ENGLISH], the Hebrew school, our second language was-- was English.
So I had English for two years.
And when we lived in Munich, Germany, all the packages that we got, care packages-- at that time, people didn't have food.
It was hunger.
It was awful.
It was right after the war.
We were able to befriend a professor, a woman professor from Oxford University.
And she came to the house, and she taught us English and German-- she was German.
And we paid her with care packages.
A carton of cigarettes, coffee, bacon.
Whatever was in the package I shared with her.
We also lived in Munich, in Germany.
We lived with a German family, and they were very nice to us, too.
And I also gave her all the food so she cleaned my room for me, and she cooked for me.
And we waited from day to day.
While my husband was going to school, we quite couldn't get out.
Already I was looking forward to come to America.
We came to America.
It was a tremendous disappointment again.
Freedom was beautiful.
I loved every minute of being in America.
But my husband didn't finish school, we didn't have a trade, we didn't have a profession.
It was a tremendous struggle, financial struggle.
How we survived?
In the beginning, my uncle supported us.
He was very well to do.
And he supported us.
And then my husband got some help from his brother, who was a physician.
And we both worked.
And somehow we managed.
But was very-- minimum wages.
We didn't know the language well, we were just-- and it was very strange to meet my brothers, because I didn't know them.
They were like strangers.
Right after the-- yeah, my mother corresponded with them all the time.
And I always saw pictures of them, the whole family.
But when we-- when we got to Munich, to Germany, we corresponded with them.
We were in touch with them.
They sent us-- sent us packages.
But when I came here, it was very strange.
Because I-- one brother I never knew, because I was born after him, two years he was.
In other words, when he left, I wasn't born.
I was born two years later.
Then my other brother left when I was about 5 years old.
So they were strangers.
But we quickly warmed up to each other, and there was a lot of love, and a lot of affection, and they were very happy to have me.
No.
We went to Poland a few years ago.
My husband will tell you about his friend who helped him to survive.
We went to visit him.
We were in touch with him, and we've helped them financial, and other, too.
He'll tell you about it.
Right.
That's the Righteous there.
He's alive, he's in Warsaw.
We correspond with him.
And we went to visit him, and we've had a wonderful, wonderful reunion.
He was so surprised that we came to see him.
We just-- I wasn't interested in sight seeing, I was-- we weren't interested in anything, just to spend time with him.
My husband will you tell you about him.
Yes.
My husband will tell you, because they were his friends, too.
Yes, we're in touch with them.
People in Florida, Polish people.
Yes, I started to work.
I didn't have really any particular skills, and my English wasn't adequate.
I worked in a factory for about a month.
And then I was able to get a job in Prentice Hall, at the publishing house.
And I went on the bus and it was a hardship, too.
It was also a hardship.
No, no.
In New York.
It was on Varick Street.
Yeah, I worked there.
And it was a hardship, too, because my foot was giving me a lot of trouble.
I was-- I just don't want to complain about my hard life.
It's hard.
Yeah, two years later our daughter was born.
Yes, it was a different world altogether.
I was busy with her, and I forgot about the war, about everything.
Connected with the war experiences?
Yes.
I did tell you?
No.
But when she was very young, I was always had nightmares.
It stays with me now.
I used wake up during the night screaming and crying.
I was afraid they're coming to take her away from me.
This went on for a long time.
Even my granddaughter now, too.
I'd hug my granddaughter, I felt the same feeling.
Sorry.
Nightmares and flashbacks.
Oh, I still get, but not as much as I used to.
Everything.
If I see even about Bosnia, I see experiences of the people, I get flashbacks.
Hostages, I get flashbacks.
Everything you see on the news, I get flashbacks.
Everything pertaining wars and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] yeah.
Even now during the snowstorm, To me it was like, you know, Doctor Zhivago.
And I'm reminiscing life before the war.
Because we had this type of winter all the time.
It never got warm, but we were equipped.
We had warm clothing, and we learned to live with it.
I observe all the holidays, but I'm not a Shul-goer, let's put it that way.
I don't go-- I go just to-- I'm a nationalistic Jew.
I am not really an observant Jew, but I'm very, very Jewish in my heart.
This is a question that's very difficult for me to answer, but I want to talk about it.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My father-- excuse me one minute.
Sorry.
My father was like God to me.
And after they killed my father, I was very young at the time, and I was with my-- I said, where was God?
Why God wasn't here to save my father, to save all the Jews?
Why did he kill all the rabbis, all the religious people?
And God stood still.
Where is God?
And I really lost faith.
I tried to believe, because it's a good crutch.
It helps you go through life if you believe in something.
But I observe all the holidays, all with my daughter and granddaughter to have some kind of a identity.
And I go to Temple [NON-ENGLISH] twice a year, that's Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur, and that's it.
And I'm not kosher.
I don't eat bacon.
I would love to, but it's high in cholesterol.
And I don't want to get fat.
Yes.
And what happened to 6 million?
I don't know.
It was luck.
It was will and luck.
I cannot say because we were so smart, smarter than the others.
My friends were just as smart, if not smarter.
It wasn't a question of being smart to survive.
It wasn't a question of being religious.
All the religious Jews were gone.
It was pure luck, destiny.
I'm a firm believer in.
It's a key again, I have to go to God if I believe in destiny, right?
Quite a few years ago, I met two very religious Jews in the mountains.
One reminded me of my father.
He looked very much like him, and I tried to get close to him, and they tried to move away because they didn't want to speak to a woman.
And I approached them, and I told them my experiences.
And I talked about my father, that he was a Talmudic scholar, a highly religious one.
And I told them, I wish I could go back to religion, to believe.
Because I was extremely religious.
I waited six hours from meat to dairy, and I fasted Yom Kippur.
And one of them was a survivor.
And he was silent, too.
Even though he was so observant, And maybe-- I think he was a rabbi.
He was silent.
And then later he said to me, his only answer was, try to go back.
That was his-- I was very impressed with his answer because he was a survivor of the Holocaust himself.
And he said-- he was a-- yes, I recall.
He was a rabbi of a synagogue.
Orthodox, very Orthodox.
And he said, try to go back.
If I tried?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Not really.
They didn't have bases or-- my father always wore a hat, and all his friends are rabbis.
All his friends were people from the synagogue.
And he was always-- he was always learning and studying.
Excuse me.
Yeah, I think I will.
After your interview, I will.
Never like this, no.
Just among-- none of my friends did, because they lost children, they lost their husbands.
And as matter of fact, when I started to talk, one of my friends said, what is the matter with you?
You always bring out the same thing.
Something is wrong with you.
Forget it.
And I said, well, I remember you before the war.
I thought it's something to talk about.
Now she's talking, too.
As a matter of fact, she wrote a book, and she's going to be interviewed in a few weeks.
She got a call already.
She lives in Mamaroneck.
That's my friend.
And everybody's talking now.
Because we feel we're getting older, and we're not going to live forever.
And we want the future generation to be aware what happened.
What happened with the families of the Jews.
This tape is for my daughter Lorene, and for my granddaughter Simone, and for my nieces.
They're my sister's two daughters.
One, her name is Stephanie, and the other one is Deja.
Alice Deja.
And my niece in California, Rita, and her children.
And for my niece's children, my grand-nieces.
That's what-- it's for them.
I would like them to be Jewish.
That's the message.
I want them to be aware about their religion, and their heritage.
That's the message.
I can't think right now.
I guess my husband will tell you the rest.
He'll give you different informations which will be--
Yeah, because we were parallel, right?
We were together all the time.
Oh, I'd like to mention-- maybe he'll be too modest to say.
In the ghetto when I said goodbye-- oh no, excuse me.
Before that.
My mother saw me befriending my husband.
At that time we were friends.
And she said to me, I can't begin to tell you how happy I am that you are-- that he's your boyfriend, because he came from such a nice home.
He has such a good background, so he'll be a good husband to you.
Those were the words I remember.
And then another thing is how he saved my life.
If not for him, I would've never escaped from the ghetto.
I would've gone with my mother.
He pulled me out.
And when we approached the gate, and there was this policeman guarding, he said to him, I'll let you out, but I can't let both-- I can't let you both out.
I have to go back.
And then we went back.
Both of us.
And later, he found another one, and the other policeman let us out.
He saved my life.
But then I also saved his life with the [NON-ENGLISH] because he had no place.
Finished?
